intro:
Samuel (Hebrew Shemu'el, understood as "heard of God" or "asked of God") was the last of Israel's judges and the first of its great prophets — the pivotal figure who presided over the nation's passage from the era of the judges to the monarchy. Born to Elkanah and to Hannah, who had been barren, he was the answer to her anguished prayer; she vowed to give him to the LORD, and at his weaning brought him to the sanctuary at Shiloh to serve under Eli the priest (1 Samuel 1:11, 20, 27–28). Hannah's song of praise at his dedication (1 Samuel 2:1–10) anticipates the themes — God exalting the lowly and judging the proud — that would mark his life and later echo in the Magnificat.

As a boy ministering before the LORD, Samuel received the divine call in a night vision, answering at Eli's counsel, "Speak, for your servant hears" (1 Samuel 3:9–10). From then "the LORD was with him and let none of his words fall to the ground," and all Israel from Dan to Beersheba knew him as an established prophet (1 Samuel 3:19–20). After the ark was lost and Eli's house fell under judgment, Samuel called the nation to repentance and led its spiritual and military recovery at Mizpah, where the Philistines were routed and he raised the stone Ebenezer — "Till now the LORD has helped us" (1 Samuel 7:3–12). He judged Israel all his days on a circuit between Bethel, Gilgal, Mizpah, and his home at Ramah (1 Samuel 7:15–17).

When the elders demanded a king "like all the nations," Samuel warned them that they were rejecting the LORD's own kingship, yet at God's direction he anointed Saul son of Kish as Israel's first king (1 Samuel 8:4–9; 1 Samuel 10:1). After Saul's disobedience, Samuel pronounced his rejection — "to obey is better than sacrifice" (1 Samuel 15:22–23) — and was sent to Bethlehem to anoint the young David, the man after God's own heart (1 Samuel 16:13). In his farewell address he challenged the people to testify that he had defrauded no one, vindicating a lifetime of integrity (1 Samuel 12:1–5). After his death and burial at Ramah, his form was summoned through the medium at Endor to pronounce Saul's doom (1 Samuel 28:11–19).

Samuel's significance lies in his unique threefold office as judge, priest-like intercessor, and prophet at the hinge of Israel's history. Scripture remembers him as a mighty man of prayer, ranked with Moses among those who stood in the breach for the people (Psalm 99:6; Jeremiah 15:1), and the New Testament names him the head of the prophetic line that pointed forward to Christ — "all the prophets who have spoken, from Samuel and those who came after" proclaimed these days (Acts 3:24; Acts 13:20; Hebrews 11:32). As the prophet who anointed David and announced an obedient kingship under God's word, he stands among the great forerunners of the Messiah, great David's greater Son.
hitchcock_meaning: heard of God; asked of God
